Threat.value: Irritable behavior, angry outbursts, or acting aggressively
Taking too many risks or doing things that could cause you harm
Being superalert or watchful or on guard

Memory.value: My memories of the frightening event are like a film that plays over and over.
When I remember the frightening event I feel like it is happening right now.
My memories of the frightening event are very clear and detailed.

Negative Appraisals.value: I feel like I don't know myself anymore
I will never be able to feel normal emotions again

Maladaptive Strategies.value: Going to or near the place where the event happened
I release my tension with sexual fantasies/ activities
Being in circumstances that remind me of the event (eg, being in the dark).

vignette:
This 63-year-old North American non-binary person, who is married, presents with persistent posttraumatic symptoms following a military trauma. Their PCL-5 score of 35 is reflected in a life that has narrowed around repeated intrusions, persistent sense of threat, and a growing estrangement from self. Crowded spaces, shouting in Arabic, and certain songs reliably set off a surge of distress. In those moments, their body moves quickly into a state of alertness, with irritability, angry outbursts, and a tendency to act in ways that feel risky or unnecessarily forceful. They describe being “superalert,” scanning the environment and struggling to settle even in ordinary daily settings.

The trauma is experienced not as a past event but as something that returns with striking force. They report that memories of the frightening event play over and over like a film, and when they recall it they feel as though it is happening right now. These recollections are vivid and detailed, leaving little distance between then and now. The intensity of the memory keeps the event mentally present, and their sense of danger remains easily activated. The same memories also feed a bleak self-understanding, with the person describing feeling dead inside, no longer knowing themselves, and fearing they will never again feel normal emotions.

Their appraisals have become increasingly constricted and self-alienating. They often speak from a place of emotional numbness and identity loss, as though the trauma has erased something essential. This sense of diminished selfhood sits alongside ongoing threat-focused monitoring, and both shape the way they move through the world. The memory of the event, together with the current expectation of danger, reinforces these negative appraisals and keeps the trauma psychologically central.

Coping has taken a narrowing form. They avoid going to or near the place where the event happened, keep away from circumstances that remind them of it, including being in the dark, and use sexual fantasies or activities to release tension. These strategies offer short-term relief but leave the person increasingly isolated from ordinary routines and from their spouse. The overall picture is of a person whose day-to-day life is organized around managing intrusive recollections, heightened threat, and a profound sense of disconnection.
